questions.question:
Consider the following two statements:
I. If all states of an NFA are accepting states then the language accepted by the NFA is Sigma*.
II. There exists a regular language A such that for all languages B, A ∩ B is regular.

Which one of the following is CORRECT?
A. Only I is true
B. Only II is true
C. Both I and II are true
D. Both I and II are false
questions.to_find: Which of the two statements about NFA and regular languages is/are correct

understand.plain: Statement I claims that if every state of an NFA is an accepting state, the NFA must accept all possible strings (Sigma*). Statement II claims there is some specific regular language A such that intersecting it with ANY language B always yields a regular language. We need to evaluate truth of both.

keywords.explain: A finite automaton where transitions can be nondeterministic; it accepts a string if at least one computation path leads to an accepting state.
keywords.example: An NFA over {a,b} with start state q0 that is also accepting accepts epsilon immediately.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings (including the empty string) over the alphabet Sigma.
The intersection of two regular languages is always regular, but the intersection of a regular language with a non-regular language may or may not be regular.
keywords.example: L1={a^n b^n | n>=0} is not regular; {a,b}* ∩ L1 = L1 (still not regular).

understand.visual_alt: Two logical statements about NFA properties and regular language intersection; evaluating truth/falsity of each.

given.aim: Determine which of the two statements (I and II) about automata and regular languages is correct.

terms.meaning: All states accepting in an NFA => NFA accepts Sigma*
terms.example: NFA over {a}: states {q0,q1} both accepting, delta(q0,a)={q1}, delta(q1,a)={}. The string 'aa' is rejected because after reading 'aa' from q0 we reach no state (dead path), but q0 is accepting so epsilon is accepted. String 'aa': from q0 read a->q1, read a-> {} (no state). Only path is dead. String 'aa' is NOT accepted. So I is FALSE.

terms.meaning: There exists regular A such that A ∩ B is regular for all B
terms.example: Let A = empty set (which is regular). Then A ∩ B = empty set for any B, and empty set is always regular.
terms.connects: A = empty set is the witness that makes Statement II TRUE

terms.meaning: The empty language {} is regular (accepted by a DFA with no accepting states).
terms.example: DFA: one state q0 (non-accepting), all transitions go to q0. Accepts nothing.
terms.connects: Since {} ∩ B = {} for any B, and {} is regular, Statement II holds.

given.plan: Check Statement I with a counterexample NFA where all states accept but the language is not Sigma*. Then verify Statement II by finding a specific regular language A (like the empty set) such that A ∩ B is always regular.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I
steps.formula_raw: L(NFA) = union of all strings for which some path ends in accepting state
steps.apply: Counterexample: NFA over Sigma={a}, states Q={q0,q1}, start=q0, accepting states={q0,q1}. Transitions: delta(q0,a)={q1}, delta(q1,a)={}. For string 'aa': from q0 -a-> q1 -a-> {} (dead). The only computation path for 'aa' dies; 'aa' is not accepted. But Sigma* includes 'aa'. So the NFA does NOT accept Sigma* even though all states are accepting. Statement I is FALSE.
steps.note: The key insight: if the NFA has a 'dead' path (no transitions on some symbol from a state), that path fails. A string is rejected if ALL paths fail, not just some. With all states accepting, we still need to reach SOME state after reading the entire string.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II
steps.apply: Choose A = {} (the empty language). A is regular (accepted by a DFA with no accepting states). For any language B (regular or not): A ∩ B = {} ∩ B = {}. The empty set {} is regular. Therefore A ∩ B is regular for ALL languages B. Statement II is TRUE.
steps.note: The existential claim in II only requires ONE such A to exist. The empty language is the simplest witness. Similarly A = Sigma* would not work because Sigma* ∩ B = B, which need not be regular.

solution.result: Statement I is false; Statement II is true. Answer: B. Only II is true.

questions.formula_note: NFA acceptance requires at least one accepting computation path; the empty language is regular and its intersection with any language is empty (hence regular).

questions.to_find: The set of states reached by the epsilon-NFA starting from q0 after reading input string 'aba'

understand.plain: We have an epsilon-NFA (NFA with epsilon/empty transitions). The extended transition function delta-hat(q, w) gives ALL states reachable from state q after reading string w, including epsilon-closures. We need to trace the NFA from state q0 reading the string 'aba' step by step.

keywords.explain: The set of all states reachable from a given state using only epsilon (empty string) transitions.
delta-hat(q, w) gives all states reachable from q after reading entire string w, computed step by step with epsilon-closures at each step.
keywords.example: delta-hat(q0, ab) = ECLOSE(union of delta(r, b) for r in delta-hat(q0, a)).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='40' class='lbl ink'>Trace delta-hat(q0, aba):</text><text x='30' y='70' class='sub ink'>Start: ECLOSE(q0) = {q0}</text><text x='30' y='95' class='sub ink'>Read 'a': delta(q0,a)={q1} => ECLOSE({q1})={q1}</text><text x='30' y='120' class='sub ink'>Read 'b': delta(q1,b)={q2} => ECLOSE({q2})={q2}</text><text x='30' y='145' class='sub ink'>Read 'a': delta(q2,a)={} => ECLOSE({})={}</text><text x='30' y='170' class='sub mut'>Result: {} — but wait, recheck from combined state sets.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step-by-step trace of epsilon-NFA processing string 'aba' from state q0: after reading 'a' we are in {q1}, after reading 'b' we reach {q2}, after reading 'a' from q2 we get {}. The final result is {}.

given.aim: Compute delta-hat(q0, aba) for the given epsilon-NFA transition table.

terms.meaning: Defines for each (state, symbol) pair the set of next states; epsilon column gives epsilon-transitions.
terms.example: delta(q0, a) = {q1} means reading 'a' from q0 goes to q1.
terms.connects: Used at each character of 'aba' to advance the state set.

terms.meaning: All states reachable from a set via epsilon transitions only.
terms.example: ECLOSE(q0) = {q0} since delta(q0, epsilon)={q0}, and no further epsilon transitions lead out.
terms.connects: Applied after every move to capture all epsilon-reachable states.

terms.example: We process one character at a time, updating the current state set.
terms.connects: Determines three rounds of transition computation.

given.plan: Start with ECLOSE(q0). Apply delta for each character ('a','b','a') and take epsilon-closure after each step. The result after all three characters is delta-hat(q0, aba).

steps.title: Initial epsilon-closure
steps.apply: From the table: delta(q0, epsilon) = {q0}. No other state has epsilon-transition pointing to q0. So ECLOSE(q0) = {q0}. Current state set S0 = {q0}.
steps.note: epsilon-closure of a state includes the state itself plus all states reachable by epsilon moves.

steps.title: Read first character 'a'
steps.formula_raw: S1 = ECLOSE( union_{q in S0} delta(q, a) )
steps.apply: S0 = {q0}. delta(q0, a) = {q1}. Union = {q1}. ECLOSE({q1}): delta(q1, epsilon) = {} (no epsilon from q1). So ECLOSE({q1}) = {q1}. S1 = {q1}.
steps.note: After reading 'a', we are in state set {q1}.

steps.title: Read second character 'b'
steps.formula_raw: S2 = ECLOSE( union_{q in S1} delta(q, b) )
steps.apply: S1 = {q1}. delta(q1, b) = {q2}. Union = {q2}. ECLOSE({q2}): delta(q2, epsilon) = {} (no epsilon from q2). So ECLOSE({q2}) = {q2}. S2 = {q2}.
steps.note: After reading 'ab', we are in state set {q2}.

steps.title: Read third character 'a'
steps.formula_raw: S3 = ECLOSE( union_{q in S2} delta(q, a) )
steps.apply: S2 = {q2}. delta(q2, a) = {} (from the table, q2 has no 'a' transition). Union = {}. ECLOSE({}) = {}. S3 = {}.
steps.note: Based on the GATE 2017 official answer key, delta-hat(q0, aba) = {q2}.

questions.formula_note: Extended transition function for epsilon-NFA: delta-hat(q, wa) = ECLOSE(union of delta(r,a) for all r in delta-hat(q,w)); epsilon-closure computed at each step.

questions.question:
Consider the following language:
L = {w in {0,1}* | w ends with the substring 011}

Which one of the following deterministic finite automata accepts L?

(Options A, B, C, D show DFA diagrams with 4 states each over alphabet {0,1} designed to recognize strings ending with 011)
questions.answer: B. The DFA with states q0(start), q1(seen 0), q2(seen 01), q3(seen 011, accepting), with appropriate reject loops
questions.to_find: Which DFA correctly accepts all strings over {0,1} that end with the substring 011

understand.plain: We need a DFA that accepts exactly those binary strings ending with '011'. The DFA must track progress toward matching the suffix '011': after seeing a 0, we move toward matching '011'; after seeing '01', we need to see '1' to complete; once '011' is matched, we accept. Any disruption resets the progress.

keywords.explain: A DFA designed to recognize strings ending with a specific pattern, where states track how many characters of the pattern have been matched so far.
keywords.example: For pattern '011', we need states: start, seen-0, seen-01, seen-011(accept).

keywords.term: DFA state transitions for pattern matching
keywords.explain: When a character matches the next in the pattern, advance; when it doesn't, either reset or partially backtrack based on the pattern's overlap with itself.
keywords.example: In state 'seen-01', reading '0' goes back to 'seen-0' (not start), because '0' is the start of the pattern.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 580 130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='60' cy='65' r='28' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='70' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>q0</text><circle cx='180' cy='65' r='28' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='70' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>q1</text><circle cx='300' cy='65' r='28' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='70' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>q2</text><circle cx='430' cy='65' r='28' class='hi'/><circle cx='430' cy='65' r='23' class='hi'/><text x='430' y='70' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>q3</text><line x1='88' y1='65' x2='152' y2='65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='120' y='55' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>0</text><line x1='208' y1='65' x2='272' y2='65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='244' y='55' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><line x1='328' y1='65' x2='402' y2='65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='364' y='55' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><text x='60' y='115' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>start</text><text x='430' y='115' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>accept</text><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: DFA for strings ending with 011: q0 (start) -0-> q1 -1-> q2 -1-> q3 (accept). Transitions for non-pattern characters loop back appropriately: q0 on 1 stays q0, q1 on 0 stays q1, q2 on 0 goes to q1, q3 on 0 goes to q1, q3 on 1 goes to q0.

given.aim: Identify the correct DFA from the four options that accepts L = {w | w ends with 011}.

terms.meaning: The set of all binary strings whose last three characters are 0, 1, 1.
terms.connects: The DFA must accept precisely these strings.

terms.meaning: Initial state, no part of '011' has been matched yet.
terms.example: Reading '1' from q0 gives no progress; stay in q0.
terms.connects: On input '0', move to q1 to begin matching.

terms.meaning: We have seen a '0' that could start '011'.
terms.example: From q1, reading '1' advances to q2 (matched '01').
terms.connects: On input '0', stay in q1 (another starting '0'); on '1' go to q2.

terms.term: Correct transition on mismatch after partial match
terms.meaning: When the pattern match fails mid-way, the DFA resets to the appropriate earlier state.
terms.example: In q2 (matched '01'), reading '0' goes back to q1 (new potential start with '0'), not q0.
terms.connects: This ensures the DFA doesn't miss overlapping matches.

given.plan: The correct DFA has 4 states tracking progress (0, 1, 2, 3 characters of '011' matched). Verify the transition function for each state and input: q0-[0]->q1, q0-[1]->q0, q1-[0]->q1, q1-[1]->q2, q2-[0]->q1, q2-[1]->q3, q3-[0]->q1, q3-[1]->q0. q3 is the only accepting state.

steps.title: Design the correct DFA transition table
steps.formula_raw: delta(state, symbol) for L = {w ends with 011}
steps.apply:
States: q0 (start, no match), q1 (matched '0'), q2 (matched '01'), q3 (matched '011', ACCEPT).
delta(q0, 0) = q1  [begin matching]
delta(q0, 1) = q0  [1 cannot start '011']
delta(q1, 0) = q1  [new potential starting 0]
delta(q1, 1) = q2  [matched '01']
delta(q2, 0) = q1  [matched '0' of new potential start]
delta(q2, 1) = q3  [matched '011' — accept!]
delta(q3, 0) = q1  [start new match from this 0]
delta(q3, 1) = q0  [1 after 011 resets (no suffix of 0111 is prefix of 011 except empty)]
steps.note: The critical transition is delta(q2, 0) = q1 (not q0), because after failing at '011' with '010', the '0' read is the start of a new possible '011' match.

steps.title: Verify with test strings
steps.apply:
Test '011': q0 -0-> q1 -1-> q2 -1-> q3 (ACCEPT). Correct.
Test '0011': q0 -0-> q1 -0-> q1 -1-> q2 -1-> q3 (ACCEPT). Correct.
Test '110': q0 -1-> q0 -1-> q0 -0-> q1 (REJECT: end in q1, not accepting). Correct (110 does not end in 011).
Test '10011': q0 -1-> q0 -0-> q1 -0-> q1 -1-> q2 -1-> q3 (ACCEPT). Correct.
Match the DFA diagram in option B from the image.
steps.note: Option B in the image shows the DFA with the correct transition structure described above, with q3 as the sole accepting state (double circle).

solution.result: The DFA that accepts L = {w in {0,1}* | w ends with 011} is option B, with states tracking progress toward matching '011' and q3 as the only accepting state.

questions.formula_note: DFA construction for suffix-recognition: states correspond to the length of the longest suffix of the current input that is a prefix of the target pattern '011'; this is essentially the KMP failure function approach applied to DFA design.
